course: Algorithms (EE4033-01), 113-2 (Spring 2025), Lecture 11 — Dijkstra's Algorithm, Shao-Hua Sun, EE @ NTU

prerequisites: Weighted directed graphs and adjacency-list representation
candidates.why_it_matters: Core topic of the lecture: computes minimum-weight paths from a source s to every other vertex in a graph with non-negative edge weights by maintaining a set S of finalized vertices, repeatedly extracting the minimum-estimate vertex from Q, adding it to S, and relaxing its outgoing edges.
candidates.transcript_summary: Pseudocode (slide 2): Initialize-Single-Source(G,s); S=Φ; Q=G.V; while Q≠Φ: u=Extract-Min(Q); S=S∪{u}; for each v∈Adj[u]: Relax(u,v,w). Greedy choice: always pick the vertex nearest the source; stated to execute essentially the same as Prim's algorithm.

prerequisites: Distance estimate v.d and predecessor v.π
candidates.why_it_matters: Relaxation is the atomic update that drives shortest-path estimates monotonically downward: if v.d > u.d + w(u,v), set v.d = u.d + w(u,v) and v.π = u. Initialization sets all v.d = ∞, v.π = NIL, and s.d = 0. Mastering relaxation is essential to understanding the whole algorithm and reconstructing paths via predecessors.
candidates.transcript_summary: Relax(u,v,w) (slide 4): 1. if v.d > u.d + w(u,v) 2. v.d = u.d + w(u,v) 3. v.π = u. Initialize-Single-Source(G,s): v.d=∞, v.π=NIL for all v; s.d=0.

candidates.concept: Step-by-step execution trace on an example graph
candidates.why_it_matters: Two full slides (Example and Practice) trace the algorithm vertex-by-vertex, visualizing S (explored, black), the vertex u being processed (gray), vertices still in Q = V−S (white), distance labels, and predecessor edges. This trace is the primary pedagogical vehicle and is ideal for an interactive, animated visualization.
candidates.transcript_summary: Example graph (slide 3): vertices s,t,x,y,z with weights 10,5,2,3,1,9,7,4,6,2; distances evolve from ∞ to final s=0, y=5, t=8, z=7, x=9, with predecessor edges highlighted. Practice graph (slide 4): vertices s,a,b,c,d,e for hands-on tracing, final labels a=8,b=5,c=9,d=7,e=10.

prerequisites: Linear array, binary heap, and Fibonacci heap
candidates.why_it_matters: The running time depends entirely on the priority-queue implementation: O(V^2) with a linear array, O(E lg V) with a binary heap, and O(E + V lg V) amortized with a Fibonacci heap. This ties algorithm design to data-structure choice and can be demonstrated empirically by timing array- vs. heap-based runs on growing graphs.
candidates.transcript_summary: Slide 5: linear array — Extract-Min O(V) → O(V^2) over the loop, relaxation O(E)×O(1). Binary heap — Extract-Min O(lg V) → O(V lg V), relaxation O(E)×O(lg V) for Decrease-Key → O(E lg V). Fibonacci heap — amortized O(E + V lg V).

candidates.concept: Correctness proof (loop invariant and induction on |S|)
candidates.why_it_matters: Establishes why the greedy choice is correct: the loop invariant says that for every v ∈ S, v.d = δ(s,v). The inductive argument depends critically on non-negative edge weights — any alternative path P must leave S and is already at least as long as P_v by the time it does. This also explains why Dijkstra fails with negative edges.
candidates.transcript_summary: Slide 6: loop invariant 'for each v∈S, v.d=δ(s,v)'. Induction on |S|: basis |S|=1; inductive step grows S by v via final edge (u,v); P_u is shortest s-u path; any other s-v path P leaves S at first non-S node y (with x∈S just before); w(P_v)=v.d=u.d+w(u,v) ≤ x.d+w(x,y) ≤ w(P).

candidates.concept: Relationship between Dijkstra's and Prim's algorithms
candidates.why_it_matters: Both are greedy, both use a min-priority queue with Extract-Min and key updates, and differ only in the update rule (path distance u.d + w(u,v) vs. raw edge weight w(u,v)). Contrasting them deepens understanding of greedy graph algorithms.
candidates.transcript_summary: Slide 2 shows MST-Prim(G,w,r) pseudocode beside Dijkstra and states Dijkstra 'executes essentially the same as Prim's algorithm.' Prim sets v.key = w(u,v); Dijkstra relaxes v.d = u.d + w(u,v).

selected_concepts: Step-by-step execution trace on an example graph
Correctness proof (loop invariant and induction on |S|)
selection_rationale: Selection follows the lecture's own structure and the project's quality priorities (executability, concept correctness, interactivity, visualization). The main loop, relaxation, and the worked execution trace form the irreducible core and are maximally demo-feasible: a Colab notebook can implement Dijkstra and animate the example/practice graphs (distance labels, S vs. Q, predecessor edges) for high interactivity and visualization value. Time complexity is selected because it is a full slide and supports an executable empirical comparison of linear-array vs. binary-heap implementations, linking algorithm to data structure. The correctness proof is selected for pedagogical completeness — it explains the non-negative-weight requirement and can be illustrated with a negative-edge counterexample — though its demo feasibility is lower since it is primarily mathematical. The Dijkstra-vs-Prim relationship remains a candidate only: a single contrasting remark of lower importance that would dilute focus, recorded for optional inclusion.
assumptions: The single PDF (6 slides) is the complete source material for this run; no separate instructor transcript was provided, so transcript_summary fields are derived from slide text and figures.
Handwritten annotations in the Example/Practice figures (e.g., 'Relax(s,y,w)', '5+3=8') are instructor working notes confirming the trace, not additional concepts.
Final example distances are read from the figures as s=0, y=5, t=8, z=7, x=9, and are intended to be reproduced exactly by the demo implementation.
Graphs are directed with non-negative weights, consistent with the stated goal and the correctness proof.
